## Global

# Global Preferences

These preferences apply across all projects.

## Code Style
- Prefer clear, readable code over clever one-liners
- Use meaningful variable and function names
- Keep functions small and focused — single responsibility
- Add comments only when the *why* is not obvious from the code

## Communication
- Be concise in explanations
- Show code changes rather than describing them
- When suggesting alternatives, explain trade-offs briefly

## Project Context
- Author: Akarshan Arora
- Domain: VLSI Engineering & Web Development
- Common tech: TypeScript, Svelte, Astro, React, Python

## Active Projects

| Project | Stack | Purpose |
|---------|-------|---------|
| pdn-from-zero | SvelteKit, mdsvex, Tailwind | Interactive PDN education site |
| yaml-from-zero | SvelteKit, mdsvex, Tailwind, Shiki | Interactive YAML education site |
| pdn-toy-tools | SvelteKit, Three.js, D3 | PDN visualization tools |
| portfolio | Astro, React, GSAP | Personal portfolio site |
| remotion-videos | Remotion, React, Three.js | Educational video creation |
| vlsi-engineering-books | Sphinx, MyST, Python | VLSI engineering book collection |
| misc | Any | Catch-all for general learnings, tooling, small projects |

# Knowledge Vault — Auto-Capture Rules

A knowledge vault lives at `~/llm-wiki/`. You MUST update it during sessions that produce durable learnings. This is not optional.

## When to Write

Write to the vault when any of these occur during a session:
- A **bug or gotcha** is discovered and fixed (especially non-obvious ones)
- A **pattern or technique** is learned that has future reuse value
- An **architectural decision** is made with trade-offs worth recording
- A **tool quirk or workaround** is found (build tools, libraries, APIs)
- A **debugging approach** worked (or didn't) in a useful way
- A **configuration** took effort to get right

Do NOT wait until the end of the session. Write at **natural checkpoints** — once a learning is confirmed and stable, record it. Multiple vault updates per session are normal and expected.

Skip vault edits ONLY if: the session is purely read-only/review, no durable learning was produced, or the user explicitly says not to.

## Where to Write

The vault is organized by project:

```
~/llm-wiki/
├── projects/{project}/             ← Project-specific knowledge
│   ├── {topic}.md                  ← Topic pages (architecture, tools, patterns)
│   └── patterns/{category}.md      ← Error patterns, gotchas, fixes
├── projects/misc/                  ← Catch-all: general tooling, Linux, git, small projects
├── shared/                         ← Cross-project knowledge
└── projects/general/global.md      ← This file (global preferences)
```

**Routing rules:**
- If the learning is specific to the current project → write to `~/llm-wiki/projects/{project}/`
- If it applies across projects → write to `~/llm-wiki/shared/`
- If you're not in a recognized project, or the learning is general (Linux, git, shell, tooling) → write to `~/llm-wiki/projects/misc/`
- For bug/gotcha patterns → write to `~/llm-wiki/projects/{project}/patterns/`

## How to Write

### For gotchas and error patterns (add to `patterns/*.md`):
```markdown
## Short Descriptive Title
What happened, why it was confusing, and the fix.
**Fix**: The specific solution or workaround.
**Applies when**: Context for when this matters.
```

### For general knowledge (add to or create topic pages):
- Add to an existing topic page if one fits
- Create a new `.md` file if the topic is new
- Use `wiki-links` to cross-reference other pages
- Add `#tags` in frontmatter for discoverability

### After writing to the vault:
Run the generator **once** after your last vault edit of the session:
```bash
python3 ~/llm-wiki/generate-instructions.py
```
This compiles wiki pages into each project's `.github/copilot-instructions.md`. The script uses absolute paths and works from any directory.

Do NOT run the generator after every small edit — batch edits and regenerate once before concluding vault work for the session.

## Access Note
The vault (`~/llm-wiki/`) may be outside the current workspace. If file access is restricted or denied, include the learning in your response text so the user can manually add it.

##  Moc

# Portfolio — Map of Content

Personal portfolio site for Akarshan Arora (VLSI Engineer & Developer). Built with Astro 6 + React islands, featuring cinematic animations, particle effects, and circuit board visuals.

## Architecture
- astro-architecture — Pages, components, config, build
- react-islands — React components in Astro (`client:only`)
- gsap-animation — GSAP + ScrollTrigger animations

## Design
- responsive-design — Layout, smooth scrolling, custom UX
- visual-effects — Particle canvas, circuit board, wave dividers

## Patterns & Learnings
- patterns/animation — Animation-related patterns and fixes
- patterns/layout — Layout and responsive design patterns

## Quick Reference
- **Dev server**: `bun run dev`
- **Build**: `bun run build`
- **Preview**: `bun run preview`
- **Pages**: `src/pages/`
- **Components**: `src/components/`
- **Data**: `src/data/portfolio.json`
- **Styles**: `src/styles/global.css`

## Astro Architecture

# Astro Architecture

## Project Structure
```
src/
├── pages/
│   └── index.astro         ← Main (single-page) portfolio
├── components/
│   ├── CircuitBoard.astro   ← SVG circuit board background
│   ├── WaveDivider.astro    ← SVG wave section dividers
│   └── ParticleCanvas.tsx   ← React particle effect (client:only)
├── data/
│   └── portfolio.json       ← All portfolio content (projects, experience, etc.)
└── styles/
    └── global.css           ← Global styles (~850 lines)
```

## Astro Config
- `astro.config.mjs` — React integration (`@astrojs/react`)
- Node >= 22.12.0 required
- Bun as package manager

## Key Concepts
- **Island Architecture**: Static HTML with hydrated React islands
- **`client:only="react"`**: Used for browser-only components (ParticleCanvas)
- **Content in JSON**: All portfolio data centralized in `portfolio.json`
- **Single-page design**: Everything on `index.astro` with scroll-based sections

## Gsap Animation

# GSAP Animation

## Setup
- GSAP + ScrollTrigger for scroll-driven animations
- Lenis for smooth scrolling behavior
- Animations defined in `index.astro` `<script>` blocks

## ScrollTrigger Patterns
- Trigger animations as sections enter viewport
- Pin sections for extended scroll interactions
- Scrub animations tied to scroll progress

## Lenis Smooth Scroll
- Provides buttery smooth scrolling UX
- Integrated with GSAP's ScrollTrigger for accurate triggering
- Handles momentum and easing

## Animation Guidelines
- Keep animations purposeful — enhance understanding, don't distract
- Use `will-change` CSS property sparingly for GPU-accelerated elements
- Test on mobile — reduce or disable heavy animations on small screens
- Respect `prefers-reduced-motion` media query

## Common Gotchas

## Hero elements flicker on load — fromTo + scrub conflict
Using `gsap.fromTo()` for both entrance animation AND scroll parallax on the same element causes a double-render flicker. The scroll parallax `fromTo` immediately applies its "from" values (opacity: 1), overriding the entrance animation's initial state (opacity: 0).
**Fix**: Add `immediateRender: false` to the scroll parallax `fromTo`. This prevents it from applying "from" values at creation time, while still allowing the scrub to reverse properly on scroll-up.
**Applies when**: Any element has both a timeline entrance animation and a scroll-triggered parallax.

## Hero elements disappear when scrolling back up
Changing a scroll `fromTo` to `gsap.to()` captures the CSS initial value (opacity: 0) as the start. Scrolling back up restores to opacity: 0 — permanently hidden.
**Fix**: Keep `fromTo` (so GSAP knows both start and end values for scrubbing) but add `immediateRender: false`.
**Applies when**: Elements that start hidden via CSS and are revealed by a timeline, then have scroll parallax.

## SVG animations not visible — CSS clip-path approach fails
Using CSS `clip-path: inset(0 100% 0 0)` with CSS transitions doesn't work reliably alongside GSAP ScrollTrigger.
**Fix**: Drive all SVG animations through GSAP directly. Use `getTotalLength()` for stroke-dashoffset draw effects. Don't mix CSS transitions with GSAP on the same elements.
**Applies when**: SVG line-draw or reveal animations on the portfolio.

## React Islands

# React Island Components

## Overview
React components run as hydrated islands within the static Astro site.

## ParticleCanvas
- Location: `src/components/ParticleCanvas.tsx`
- Interactive particle effect on the hero section
- Uses HTML Canvas API with `requestAnimationFrame`
- Must use `client:only="react"` (no SSR — relies on `window`/`canvas`)
- ~130 lines, handles resize, mouse interaction, particle physics

## Integration Pattern
```astro
---
import ParticleCanvas from '../components/ParticleCanvas.tsx';
---
<ParticleCanvas client:only="react" />
```

## Guidelines
- Only use React for components that need rich interactivity or Canvas
- Prefer Astro components (`.astro`) for everything else
- `client:only` means no server rendering — component only loads in browser
- Keep React islands small and self-contained

---

## Responsive Design

# Responsive Design

## Layout Strategy
- Single-page scroll design with distinct sections
- Full-viewport hero with particle canvas background
- Section dividers using SVG wave components
- Circuit board visual as decorative background element

## Custom UX Elements
- Custom cursor effects
- Loading states/transitions
- Scroll-driven section reveals

## Responsive Approach
- Mobile-first CSS with Tailwind-style breakpoints
- Global CSS in `src/styles/global.css` (~850 lines)
- Heavy use of CSS custom properties for theming
- Flexible grid layouts for project cards and content sections

## Visual Effects
- `ParticleCanvas.tsx` — interactive particle field (React/Canvas)
- `CircuitBoard.astro` — SVG circuit board pattern
- `WaveDivider.astro` — SVG wave section separators

## Typescript Patterns

# TypeScript Patterns

Shared TypeScript patterns across projects.

## Strict Mode
- Enable `strict: true` in all projects
- Avoid `any` — use `unknown` + type guards instead

## Type Patterns
- Prefer `interface` for object shapes, `type` for unions/intersections
- Use discriminated unions for state machines
- `satisfies` operator for type checking without widening

## Import Patterns
- Use path aliases (`$lib`, `@/`) for clean imports
- Barrel exports from `index.ts` for library modules

## Error Handling
- Use `Result<T, E>` pattern or explicit error types over thrown exceptions
- Type-narrow errors in catch blocks: `if (error instanceof SpecificError)`

## Common Gotchas
<!-- Add learnings here as they occur -->

## Tailwind Css

# Tailwind CSS Patterns

Shared Tailwind patterns across projects.

## Version Notes
- Most projects use Tailwind CSS v4 with the Vite plugin (`@tailwindcss/vite`)
- v4 uses CSS-first configuration (no `tailwind.config.js`)
- Import via `@import "tailwindcss"` in CSS

## Typography Plugin
- `@tailwindcss/typography` for prose content (lessons, articles)
- Apply with `prose` class on content containers
- Customize with `prose-invert` for dark themes

## Responsive Patterns
- Mobile-first approach
- Key breakpoints: `sm:640px`, `md:768px`, `lg:1024px`, `xl:1280px`
- Use container queries where appropriate

## Dark Mode
- Use `dark:` variant for dark mode styles
- Consider `prefers-color-scheme` for system preference
